[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getInClauseSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged user pass an `inConditions` array where `condition.value` is an array itself (e.g., `[1, 2, 3]`), causing `tokenIdInParams.push(condition.value)` to push an array into `tokenIdInParams`, and the resulting `${tokenIdInParams}` interpolation to produce `1,2,3` (JavaScript array-to-string coercion) — effectively injecting multiple unparameterized values into the SQL IN clause and bypassing intended single-token filtering?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getTokenRelationshipsQuery()] Can a no-permission attacker supply `conditions` with a `key` value that resolves to a column not in the SELECT list (e.g., `custom_fee_amount`) via `TokenAccount.getFullName()`, causing the query to filter on fee-related columns that are not intended to be user-filterable, effectively allowing the attacker to probe fee schedule values through boolean-based inference?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: putTokenCache()] Can an unprivileged user who can influence the `token` object passed to `putTokenCache()` supply a `token_id` of type string (e.g., `'300'`) when the cache uses BigInt keys, causing `tokenCache.has(tokenId)` to return false (type mismatch) and a duplicate cache entry to be created with a different `decimals` value, leading to cache key collision and incorrect fee data being served?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getCachedTokens()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that `tokenCache` is a module-level singleton (not per-request) to perform a cache poisoning attack across user sessions — by triggering a request that caches a token with wrong `decimals`, all subsequent users querying that token receive the poisoned fee-relevant data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getTokenAccounts()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the `rows.reduce((result, row) => result.add(row.token_id), new Set())` deduplication in `getTokenAccounts()` — if `row.token_id` values have inconsistent types (BigInt vs Number) across rows — to cause some token IDs to be deduplicated incorrectly, resulting in missing cache lookups and `decimals = null` for affected tokens?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: tokenRelationshipsQuery] Can an unprivileged user exploit the static `tokenRelationshipsQuery` which does not include a `decimals` column in its SELECT list, meaning `row.decimals` is always `undefined` from the initial query, and the entire decimals value (used in fee calculations) depends solely on the cache — making cache poisoning the only source of truth for fee-relevant precision data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getCachedTokens()] Can a no-permission attacker send concurrent requests for the same uncached token ID, causing multiple simultaneous executions of `super.getRows(TokenService.tokenCacheQuery, [uncachedTokenIds])` before any of them complete and populate the cache, resulting in redundant DB reads and a race condition where the last writer wins in `tokenCache.set()`, potentially storing a different `decimals` value than expected?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getInClauseSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged user pass `inConditions[].value` as `NaN` or `Infinity`, which JavaScript coerces to the strings `'NaN'` or `'Infinity'` in the template literal `${tokenIdInParams}`, producing invalid SQL like `ta.token_id in (NaN)` that may cause a DB error revealing internal query structure related to fee data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/tokenService.js -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getTokenAccounts()] Can a no-permission attacker cause `new TokenAccount(row)` to be constructed with `decimals = undefined` (when `cachedToken` exists but `cachedToken.decimals` is `undefined`) since `row.decimals = cachedToken.decimals` unconditionally assigns `undefined`, and `TokenAccount` constructor uses `tokenRelationship.decimals ?? null` which would then store `null`, misrepresenting the token's fee denomination?
